text:p.text:span: saying (the words) of Åulgi, your king:
(Apilaåa), the one I sent you to—is he not your own trusted subordinate?
Did he not receive (his) orders from your very own hands?
What is more, how could you so misunderstand the true meaning of all that he has been doing?
As far as I am concerned, you were to make the frontier territory secure as my representative,
to organize the people and keep them obedient,
and once you reached the cities of the frontier, to discern their attitudes
and to learn what their dignitaries are saying,
so that my battle cry would fill the mountains,
my mighty battle weapons fall upon the foreign lands,
and my “storm” cover over the­ homeland!
Drop (chasing) winds in the desert and robbers in the­ fields
Alt. transl.: make winds and robbers disappear from the desert!
Until you have reached Apilaåa, my
ignore all of this so that you can … your face before him (without delay)
Two texts have an additional line 16a; see commentary.
If my high commissioner had not expanded (his powers), just as I would have,
Var.: If you had not had not expanded my high commissioner’s powers as my representative.
if he had not sat on a throne on a dais placed over a fine carpet,
had not set his feet on a­ golden footstool,
had not by his very own authority appointed and removed
governors from the office of governor,
had not (punished anyone) by death or blinding,
(and) had not promoted those of his own choosing over others—
how else could he have secured the frontier?
then you will not be so set against him!
It is you who have expanded your powers so that you­ no longer understand your (own) guardsmen!
He has made you learn (the hard way) the responsibilities of those people, as well as the responsibilities of his own warriors!
If you are indeed both my most faithful retainers,
Var.: If you (sing.) are indeed my faithful retainer…
(you will listen together while) they read out this tablet before the two of you.
Viz.: have them read out aloud my inscribed tablet before your (pl.) very own eyes.
Both­ of you must come to an agreement
and secure the foundation­s of the frontier territory!

clearly denotes a ­relationship of subordination between two people but more precise­ legal and social ramifications of the word are difficult to­ define (the reading of the second sign is uncertain). Lexical equivalencies from Lu are­
12 202:4); but the word is not the same as Akkadian
administrative texts the context is­ always PN
. The term is already attested, albeit not very frequently, in pre-Sargonic texts (e.g.,
25 55: iv 3) as well as in Sargonic (
1 27 n. 3 for other examples) and Ur III sources.
Unlike the earlier examples, almost all the Ur III occurrences are found in connection with the
, that is with the highest military rank, and most of them are
6 34:11–12). Other occurrences are connected with a certain Puzur-ili, who may have headed a leather workshop (
, serves as witness in a text from Ur (
3: 43). Different from all the above is the example from an Ur III cylinder seal inscription,

, “Erra-qurad, lapidary of Ninlil, ‘subordinate’ of Enlil.” The cumulative evidence would suggest that they are stand-ins; they are intimate subordinates, perhaps “adjutants” of very important military officers but this specific meaning may be limited to Ur III times.
The word is rare in literary ­texts:
69 (1949) 204, restored from Ni. 9751 rev. 10 and Ni. 4567 rev. 18 (both
, “do (not) duck sideways before your own faithful retainer like an … acrobat.”

. The rhetorical import of these lines is open to a number of interpretations. Previous translations have taken for granted that these words refer to the messenger who brought the previous letter. Thus, P. Michalowski,
65, rendered the passage in the following

manner: “That man whom you have sent to me, he cannot­ (really) be your trusted subordinate! Surely he does not­ take his orders from your hand! How is it that you are ­unaware of what he is doing?” Upon further reflection one may suggest that in view of what follows, it might be better to assume that the
of line 3 is anticipatory, and that it refers to Apilaåa.

. This line seems to have created problems for the ancients and the manuscript tradition is confused. The somewhat redundant fully reconstructed line reflects the Nippur tradition. Both texts from Ur differ from one another; Ur1 has a unique set of variants, while Ur2 resembles the two sources of unknown origin, which probably came from the same place (X5, X6).
is used here in the same sense as
, which is, otherwise, only attested in a reconstructed lexical passage
to be a calque from Akkadian
, “my representative.” Elsewhere in CKU, as in ll. 16a and 17 below,
. The grammatical form, and hence the meaning of the ­final verb, differs in many of the manuscripts. The only Nippur ­witness to the line has
, which can be imperative or­ an abbreviation of
. ­Thus all the texts that are not from Nippur may be interpreted as “so that their (local) dignitaries­ know their orders.”

are ­uncertain even though the obvious parallelism suggests similar­ or antithetical meanings. I understand this, as well as the following two lines,­ as an admonition from the king to Aradmu forbidding him to be­ distracted by anything until he reaches Apilaåa. I also assume that the description of the activities of lawless people in the plain in ArÅ2: B1’–7’ (3) is related to this line, and contains Aradmu’s defense against this accusation, and this has influenced my understanding of the present line. The interpretation offered here is provisional at best, and is based on
(on the latter see also A. Cavigneaux and F. al-Rawi,
38). The choice of these particular readings is based on ArÅ2: B
lú la-ga lú sa-gaz-e eden si-si-ig-ga-bi níÑ-gul-bi bí-ak
Even rustlers and robbers break up the earth (for cultivation) on the (wind)-swept desert.”
Alt. transl.: I have made rustlers and robbers break up the earth (for cultivation) on the wind-swept desert.
I assume that the “rustler” and the “wind-swept desert” are oblique references to Åulgi’s rhetorical exhortation.
In Ur III and earlier documents The verb
, , usually means “to run away” when used of persons and animals, and “to be lost” when used of objects.
For a discussion of certain aspects of this verb, including its treatment in the lexical tradition, see P. Steinkeller,

In Old Babylonian literary texts, it can also mean “to vanish, disappear,” but there is no other example of the verb in the imperative or of any parallel passage. So is the king telling his grand vizier to deal with matters by making the “disappear,” or is he exhorting to “forget” such issues and move on to Apilaåa without distraction?

. As presently preserved, this line is filled with difficulties. There are only two Nippur sources: the inferior N1 and the badly preserved N10. N1 is in Baghdad and can only be collated from the field photo; the first verbal form is
; x appears to be a badly written
which may have been erased. Only the bottom parts of signs are preserved in
N10 but collation confirms the probability of reading
Ur2 is also broken but and the first preserved sign in the line appears to be
. The two unprovenanced Yale tablets X5 and X6, which are undoubtedly from the same place although probably from different hands, both have
. It is possible that the verb should be understood as
so that he can appear before you (without delay)
.” I do not understand the final verb, which is
in three sources; only the Ur2 text, which is not the best, provides TU as the root. Is this a syllabic variant (
)? Note the clustering of the variants in all three unprovenanced texts.
. All non-Nippur manuscripts add an additional line here (16a):

. It is not clear if these texts connected this line with 16 or with what follows. It is possible that the source of the interpolation—if that is indeed the case—is ÅuÅa1: 23–24 (letter 19), where the two are clearly related and mean “Thus I did command you! Why did you not act in accordance with my instructions?” although in the Åu-Sin letter the final verb is second person (
), rather than third. Even if this was the source, it appears that the new line has been reinterpreted (
) and was designed to introduce what follows.

text:note-body.text:p: The distribution of the interpolation is of interest since it is not found in the two Nippur manuscripts. Note that at present the Šu-Sin/Šarrum-bani correspondence is not attested from that city.

text:p.text:span: I would therefore consider it analytically part of the next paragraph: “
(As to your accusations), why should he not act according to my instructions?”
outside of CKU is in OB Lu C5 4 (
12 169); in both cases it is rendered as
, “office holder, intimate participant,” Note the exact same formulation in an Old Babylonian Akkadian language omen:
åar-rum be-el pa-ar-œi-im i-na-as-sà-aæ-ma be-el pa-ar-œi-im i-åa-ak-
, “the king will remove an office holder and install (another) office holder” (

P 202). This confirms that Apilaåa is in part usurping royal prerogatives, and that the monarch accepts this fact, as do the lines that follow, all of which have Akkadian analogies. Note the sequence of a perfect verbal form followed­ by a non-perfect one to indicate the sequence of action.
The fully reduplicated form of the reflexive pronoun
is unique. Note the idiomatic use of the verbs
together to denote the appointment and firing of officials; this probably reflects Akkadian usage as documented, for example, in the OB letter
a-na na-sa-æi-im ú åa-ka-nim qá-at-ka i-te-li na-sa-æu ù åa-ka-nu åa qá-ti åa qá-ti-x
to remove and install people? Removing and installing people is, in fact, only

17 225]). The use of this in CKU, as ­well as other contexts, implies that such actions can be done only­ upon orders of the king. Another example of blinding as a royal prerogative is found in an Old Babylonian omen (
, “the king shall tear out the eyes of the
(only in CH) for the act of blinding. There is one definite reference to the Ur III practice ­of blinding prisoners of war in an OB copy of a Åu-Sin ­inscription; see R. Kutscher,
). The Akkadian lexical equivalents are somewhat problematical. The Akkadian verb
“to destroy,” is clearly secondary, derived from its normal Sumerian correspondent
literally, “to touch,” is more difficult to define in such contexts. The literal translation, “to touch,” does not help us. The expression
is documented in a Mari letter (
18 929, l 10’); K. van der Toorn translates
this as “let them gouge out their eyes” (
79 [1985] 190). The same verb is attested with the tongue as an object in a few OB contracts; see M. deJong Ellis,
Note the extraordinary transmission of the crasis form (
) in all manuscripts from Nippur with the exception of N1, which was found in House F (
). Could these five tablets have come from the same house? The N1 text is not always the best, however. The “correct” form is found in three texts of unknown provenience (X2, X4, X6:
in X2). The scribe of Ur 2 may have been working from a tradition that also had a crasis form but one that was reinterpreted with
as an imitation of Ur III
, and a verb with the prefix
. For another example of crasis in this text see
in the Nippur manuscripts of l. 14.

. There is broad confusion concerning the first bound pronoun in this line, ranging from third person singular and plural to second person singular. Two texts omit it altogether. This confusion concerns both the referent of the pronoun and the understanding of
in his line. All previous modern renditions have taken this to be the abstract derivational morpheme but the use of the genitive
suggests that it is the homophonous, and etymologically related noun
that is used here in the meaning “province, responsibility,” corresponding to Akk.
5 17]), and that the tone is clearly sarcastic; see also PuÅ1: 15ff. (letter 13). Our own confusion about this may mirror ancient differences about the interpretation of this line, as
are commonly encountered in the school texts. Note, however that
can be used to refer to troops, as in
, “Do not let those cities out of your grasp, station your forces (there)!” Note also
, “My glory spread over the homeland but you brought your martial glory and power to that foreign land.”
. The predicate pronoun is second person plural in N1, N8, and X4 (
). The writers of N11 and Ur2 understood this as
, that is first person singular, addressed only to Aradmu. This is not consistently carried out in the lines that follow, although the Ur2 scribe continued the singular
forms in lines 32 and 33. The excerpt exercise adds the unrelated phrase
“if my king is agreeable,” taken from PuÅ: 16 (letter 13), or from some other letter.
The same scribe provides a syllabic rendering of
. The word is already attested in ED texts; for references and earlier literature see J. Krecher,
18 (1987) 9–12. Krecher uses this in support of an assumed word *
, “woman,” which I do not believe exists. As I see it,
, “born in the woman’s quarters (of the household).”
, “inscribed tablet;” except for ArÅ5: 12, where it is rendered in Akkadian as
letters and written instructions are referred to as
. The precise meaning of this word alludes us at present; in later lexical it is the equivalent of
(see the dictionaries) but in Ur III times it was certainly not simply a synonym of
, “tablet,” as evidenced by the basket tag
, “to call, name,” is used with the technical meaning of “to recite, read aloud,” with ablative dimensional prefix in OB school texts (F. Karahashi,

v 21–vi 2, albeit without the ablative; the king sees the goddess Nidaba in a dream, who is holding in her lap, and consulting, a lapis lazuli tablet on her lap, covered with “stars,” that is cuneiform signs. The last lines of the passage reads (v 25–vi 2):

office:text.text:p: That was, beyond any doubt, Her Majesty Nidaba,
The (instructions) for that temples construction, from those sacred ‘stars’

text:p.text:span: The tablet was collated on a cast held by the University Museum and on a field photograph that is reproduced on the accompanying compact disk. The right edge is not shown on either, and therefore one must rely on F. Ali’s hand copy, which was apparently made from the original tablet in Baghdad, for signs that are not visible on the photograph and cast.
34. The reverse. which is not included in the published hand copy, is heavily destroyedbut some signs of the last four lines can be read, ending in a double line.
Photograph available on the CDLI web site. The tablet has deteriorated since it was copied and then collated by D. Foxvog and myself.

text:note-body.text:p: This is not, properly speaking, a source of the letter. These are lines that were used to create a new one together with elements of the previous letter, and it is edited here as ArŠ1a (letter 1a). The appropriate lines are incorporated into the matrix for comparative purposes.

text:p.text:span: X2 possibly from Sippar, X5 and X6 possibly from Larsa. The Tutub provenience of X4 is doubtful (see p.
above). The clustering of variants suggests that the Yale tablets X5 and X6 must have come from the same place.
N1 and X3 collated on photographs (collations of the latter in ­H. Waetzoldt,
15 [1976] 332). Su1 and Su1 assigned here­ provisionally; they could be the opening lines of any of the letters from Åulgi to­ Aradmu. Neither could be located at the present time.

office:text.text:p: Tablet typology: compilation tablets: N1, N8, N10(?), Ur1, X1, X2, X6. The rest are all Type III, except X5 (one column with two letters), Su1, Su2, which are Type IV lentils, and X3 (oval exercise tablets with excerpts of letters 1 and 2).

text:p.text:span: , 332–33. C. Wilcke 1970 62–64 (lines 14–35), C. Wilcke 1993 65–66: translation, commentary (lines 6–26), P. Michalowski, Royal Letters 77–78.

text:note-body.text:p: The spacing of the break at the beginning of the line indicates that Ur1 had the personal name marker.

text:section.text:p: X6 oo o o o o . o
N9 and Ur2 must have had the first sign.
N6 oo o o o o . + +
Su2 +. o o o /+ o o o
N4 . o o o o o o o o o o o o o
N6 o o o o o o o o . + + o . +
Ur1 o o o o . . . . x o o x x x

X4 o o o o o o o o o o o .
X4 o o . o o o o o o o o . za +

text:p.text:span: X4 o o o o o o o o
X4 o o o o o o o o x

text:note-body.text:p: The sign is either si or dul, not dal.

text:p.text:span: Ur2 o o o o + + + + - o . + + + + bu
X4 o o o o o o o o o o o o o . .
Ur2 o o o o . hé-en-dal

X2 o o o o o o o o . + . o o . re

text:note-body.text:p: The scribe wrote _u10 right after na, realized that it had to be spaced at the end of the line, erased it and then wrote it in its proper place.

text:note-body.text:p: The scribe began to write kešdabut thought better and wrote zú over it.
